narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 66% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This model looks at key indicators like academic rankings, test scores, and background details to flag higher-risk applicants, and unfortunately, your profile raised several concerns. You ranked in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—much lower than the typical student's middle-of-the-pack position—which stood out as the strongest signal pushing the model toward predicting failure. Right behind that was your similarly low first-year ranking, followed closely by your LSAT score of 26.5, well below the average of around 37 that successful students usually have. Your low family income bracket also played a notable role in tipping the scales toward a fail prediction, as did your racial background as Black, which is underrepresented among bar passers compared to the mostly White dataset. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0, just a bit under the average 3.2, added to the concern, along with your gender as female and your plan to attend full-time—though these had smaller influences. In ranking their impact, your third-year decile mattered most, then first-year decile, LSAT, family income, race, GPA, gender, and full-time status, with every factor nudging the prediction away from passing rather than toward it. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, you're starting from lower academic deciles, a weaker LSAT, and tougher socioeconomic circumstances, which the model sees as hurdles based on historical data. The good news is some of these aren't set in stone: consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, as it's a key changeable factor that heavily influenced this decision. Building stronger study habits now could help you outperform your past deciles in future coursework or bar prep programs. Seeking scholarships or support programs aimed at underrepresented students might also address income-related barriers indirectly. With focused effort on these areas, you could shift future predictions in your favor and strengthen your path to passing the bar. Keep pushing—you have the determination to rewrite this story.
